children.name: Fringilla
children.description: The chaffinches — small, brightly-patterned finches and among the commonest birds in Europe. The Common Chaffinch is one of the most abundant wild birds on Earth and a defining presence in virtually every woodland edge, park, and garden on the continent. Their song — a confident, accelerating cascade ending in a curl — is one of the earliest and most persistent sounds of the European spring. Bramblings make one of the most spectacular avian migrations, with tens of millions funnelling through Scandinavia each autumn.

children.name: Fringilla coelebs
children.description: The Eurasian chaffinch, common chaffinch, or simply the chaffinch is a common and widespread small passerine Bird – in the finch family. The male is brightly coloured with a blue-grey cap and rust-red underparts. The female is more subdued in colouring, but both sexes have two contrasting white wing bars and white sides to the tail.

children.name: Fringilla alpicolla
children.description: A genetically distinct chaffinch population from the high Alps; proposed as a separate species.

children.name: Fringilla montalbetti
children.description: A recently split species from Corsica and Sardinia; genetically distinct.

children.name: Carduelis
children.description: The European Goldfinch and its relatives — one of the most jewel-like birds of the farmland and garden. The red-white-and-black face and vivid yellow wing-stripe make the Goldfinch instantly recognisable. It specialises in extracting seeds from thistles and teasel heads with its pointed bill — a niche no other small European bird occupies. Goldfinches were once trapped in enormous numbers for the cage-bird trade; legal protection in the 20th century allowed populations to recover substantially.

children.name: Carduelis carduelis
children.description: The European goldfinch, or simply referred to as the goldfinch, is a small passerine bird in the finch family that is native to the Palearctic zone in Europe, northern Africa, and western Asia. It has been introduced to other areas, including Australia, New Zealand, Uruguay and the United States.

children.name: Carduelis carduelis caniceps
children.description: The eastern subspecies of the goldfinch; grey head replaces the European red-and-white pattern.

children.name: Carduelis lawrencii
children.description: A grey-gold finch of California oak savannas with a tinkling song.

children.name: Spinus
children.description: Siskins — small, green-yellow finches of conifers and birches. The Eurasian Siskin is a nomadic bird, wandering across Europe in search of cone and alder crops with restless, twittering flocks. It readily visits garden feeders in winter, particularly for nyjer (thistle) seed. New World siskins include the impressive Pine Siskin and the Lawrence's Goldfinch, illustrating the wide radiation of this lineage across both hemispheres.

children.name: Spinus spinus
children.description: The Eurasian siskin is a small passerine bird in the finch family Fringillidae. It is also called the European siskin, common siskin or just siskin. Other (archaic) names include black-headed goldfinch, barley bird and aberdevine.

children.name: Spinus psaltria
children.description: The lesser goldfinch is a small finch in the genus Spinus native to the Americas.

children.name: Spinus xanthophthalmus
children.description: A striking black-and-yellow siskin from the Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Spinus lawrencii
children.description: A small grey-gold finch of California's oak savannas. Its tinkling, canary-like song and nomadic wandering make it a special find for birders.

children.name: Spinus atrata
children.description: A unique, almost entirely black siskin of the high Peruvian Andes.

children.name: Spinus spinecens
children.description: A distinctive and well-known species within its range. It inhabits a variety of habitats from lowland forests to mountain highlands, feeding on insects, seeds, and fruits.

children.name: Chloris
children.description: Greenfinches — stout, heavy-billed finches that favour gardens, farmland hedges, and scrub. The European Greenfinch was a common garden bird across much of Europe until a trichomonosis epidemic from 2005 onwards caused population crashes of up to 65% in some countries. Despite this, it remains widespread. Its distinctive descending 'dzeee' call and wheezing song are familiar sounds of suburban gardens. The bill is large and powerful enough to crack large seeds and even olive stones.

children.name: Chloris chloris
children.description: The European greenfinch or simply the greenfinch is a small passerine bird in the finch family Fringillidae.

children.name: Chloris kittlitzi
children.description: #REDIRECTBonin greenfinch {{Redirect category shell| }}.

children.name: Chloris ambianus
children.description: A greenfinch from the Philippines; distinctive black chin on the male.

children.name: Chloris monguilii
children.description: A greenfinch of the Himalayan foothills, from Nepal to Myanmar. Its plumage is more yellow than the European species.

children.name: Pyrrhula
children.description: Bullfinches — compact, rounded finches with a stubby, rounded bill and strikingly patterned plumage. The Eurasian Bullfinch male is one of the most vividly coloured small birds in Europe — a claret-red breast, jet black cap, and white rump visible as it flies away. Bullfinches are quiet and often unobtrusive; their plaintive, piping call is the most reliable signal of their presence. They have a specialised tongue and palate for processing seeds by husking them, rather than cracking them.

children.name: Pyrrhula leucogenys
children.description: A Philippine bullfinch; white cheeks and grey-pink body; inhabits montane forests.

children.name: Pyrrhula aurantia
children.description: A beautiful orange-breasted bullfinch of the Himalayan region.

children.name: Pyrrhula erythrops
children.description: A small bullfinch of Southeast Asian mountains with white-spotted wing coverts.

children.name: Pyrrhula nigra
children.description: A largely black bullfinch of the Himalayas, with only a hint of rosy colour on the breast.

children.name: Loxia
children.description: Crossbills — among the most specialised of all finches, with uniquely crossed bill tips evolved to prize open conifer cones. The crossing of the tips can go either right-over-left or left-over-right; the ratio varies by population. Crossbills are nomadic to an extreme degree, following cone crops across boreal and montane forests. In irruption years they appear far outside their normal range. They are among the very few birds that can breed in any month including mid-winter, timed to peak cone availability.

children.name: Loxia patagonica
children.description: A South American crossbill from the Andes of Chile and Argentina; feeds on Araucaria seeds.

children.name: Loxia ancora
children.description: Endemic to Hispaniola; the only Caribbean crossbill; critically endangered by deforestation.

children.name: Coccothraustes
children.description: The Hawfinch — the largest finch in Europe, with a massive, pointed, steel-grey bill capable of cracking cherry and olive stones with forces up to 50 kg. Despite its size and striking plumage — orange-brown with a black mask, blue-black wings, and a short white-tipped tail — the Hawfinch is notoriously shy and difficult to observe, keeping to high canopy in mature broadleaf woodland. In winter, small parties gather at hornbeam and wild cherry trees to crack the seeds.

children.name: Linaria
children.description: Linnets and redpolls — small, streaky brown finches of open country, heathland, and scrub. The Common Linnet's song is one of the most cheerful sounds of a sunny heath or clifftop in spring. The Twite is a montane equivalent, breeding on moorland and coastal salt-marshes. This group has declined markedly across western Europe due to changes in farming practice — specifically the loss of seed-bearing weeds from cultivated fields.

children.name: Acanthis
children.description: Redpolls — small Arctic and boreal finches with a crimson forehead spot and pink-flushed breast in breeding males. Redpoll taxonomy is disputed: some authorities treat Common, Lesser, and Arctic Redpolls as one species with extreme plumage variation, while others recognise three. They are irruptive nomads, appearing in lowland Europe in large numbers in some winters (when birch cone crops fail in Scandinavia) and almost absent in others.

children.name: Serinus
children.description: Small streaky yellow finches of open and scrubby habitats across Europe and Africa.

children.name: Serinus flavicola
children.description: A bright yellow-bellied species from East African savannas.

children.name: Crithagra
children.description: African seedeaters and canaries of savanna, scrub, and woodland.

children.name: Crithagra leucopterus
children.description: A small, streaky seedeater of rocky slopes, open scrub, and highland grassland in equatorial Africa, notable for its bold white wing patches that flash conspicuously in flight. Often encountered in small flocks feeding on grass seeds and weed seeds in disturbed areas.

children.name: Crithagra striolatus
children.description: A heavily streaked and extremely common seedeater of highland scrub, gardens, and forest edges from Ethiopia to South Africa. One of the most abundant and vocal seedeaters in East African highland towns and roadsides, its song a persistent jangle from roadside shrubs.

children.name: Crithagra reichelarum
children.description: A West African canary; named after the ornithologist Anton Reichenow.

children.name: Crithagra strigosus
children.description: A heavily streaked brown canary of South African fynbos and karoo shrublands.

children.name: Haemorhous
children.description: New World rosefinches with red-washed males, common in North American habitats.

children.name: Haemorhous purpureus
children.description: The purple finch is a bird in the finch family, Fringillidae. It breeds in the northern United States, southern Canada, and the west coast of North America.

children.name: Haemorhous aneus
children.description: A colourful finch of western Mexican forests; the male is bright yellow with black wings.

children.name: Carpodacus
children.description: Old World rosefinches of mountains and northern forests, the males richly red.

children.name: Carpodacus rhodochrous
children.description: A subtly beautiful rosefinch of Himalayan rhododendron scrub and conifer forests from Pakistan to Nepal, the male deep pink with a prominent pale supercilium. Inhabits dense montane forest between 2,500 and 4,000 m, descending to lower altitudes in severe winters.

children.name: Carpodacus thura
children.description: #REDIRECT Himalayan white-browed rosefinch.

children.name: Carpodacus rubicinoides
children.description: A rosefinch of the Tibetan plateau with heavy streaking on the underparts.

children.name: Carpodacus vernayi
children.description: A rosefinch of the northern Myanmar mountains, named after the naturalist Arthur Vernay.

children.name: Pinicola
children.description: A single large, plump boreal finch of northern conifer forests worldwide.

children.name: Leucosticte
children.description: Rosy finches — hardy montane finches of high-altitude snowfields, glaciers, and rocky alpine tundra in Asia and North America. They are among the few birds that spend the entire year above the treeline, foraging at the snow's edge for windblown seeds and invertebrates. Males develop a variable pink or rosy wash on the wings and rump.

children.name: Mycerobas
children.description: Allied grosbeaks — large, heavy-billed finches of Himalayan and Central Asian montane forests. The males are boldly patterned in black and yellow or red, and the massive bill is adapted for crushing large seeds and hard fruit stones. The genus is restricted to the montane forest zone of Asia from the western Himalayas east to China.

children.name: Eophona
children.description: Japanese and Chinese grosbeaks — large, stocky finches of East Asian broadleaf and mixed forests with massive orange-yellow bills. They are powerful seed and cherry-stone crackers, the bill providing enough force to open hard fruit kernels unavailable to smaller finches. Both species have a black head and pale grey body, making them among the most striking finches in Asia.

children.name: Hesperiphona
children.description: New World grosbeaks of North and Central American conifer forests, closely related to the Old World grosbeaks. The males are dramatically patterned in yellow and black with white wing patches. These are irruptive birds, appearing in large numbers well outside their core range in years of food scarcity.

children.name: Hesperiphona abeillei
children.description: The hooded grosbeak or Albiellé's grosbeak is a passerine bird in the family Fringillidae, the finches and euphonias. It is found in Guatemala, El Salvador, and Mexico.

children.name: Bucanetes
children.description: Trumpeter and Mongolian finches — small, sandy-coloured desert finches of arid and semi-arid regions from the Canary Islands and North Africa across the Middle East to Central Asia. Males have a pink or rosy wash on the plumage and bill. Their distinctive calls, often described as nasal or trumpet-like, carry well across open desert terrain.

children.name: Drepanis
children.description: Hawaiian mamos and the Iiwi — iconic Hawaiian honeycreepers with long, curved bills evolved for accessing the nectar of native lobeliad flowers. The Iiwi is one of the most recognisable birds in the Hawaiian archipelago; its brilliant scarlet plumage and sickle-shaped bill make it unmistakable. Hawaiian honeycreepers are now placed within Fringillidae based on genomic data.

children.name: Himatione
children.description: The Apapane — a crimson Hawaiian honeycreeper that is among the most abundant of all surviving Hawaiian forest birds. Unlike many of its relatives, the Apapane has maintained larger populations in part due to its ability to exploit a wider range of ohia habitat.

children.name: Himatione sanguinea
children.description: Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Endemic fauna of Hawaii.

children.name: Loxops
children.description: Small Hawaiian honeycreepers with a unique bill shape where the tips cross slightly.

children.name: Loxops caeruleirostris
children.description: The akekeʻe or Kauaʻi ʻākepa is a bird species in the family Fringillidae, where it is placed in the Hawaiian honeycreeper genus Loxops. It is endemic to the island of Kauaʻi where it is found in small numbers in higher elevations. Because of their similar size, shape, and unusual bill, the akekeʻe and the Hawaiʻi ʻākepa were for some time classified as a single species.

children.name: Hemignathus
children.description: Hawaiian honeycreepers with extremely long curved bills for extracting nectar from lobelioid flowers.

children.name: Hemignathus wilsoni
children.description: The ʻakiapōlāʻau, also known as the Hawaiian woodpecker, is a species of Hawaiian honeycreeper that is endemic to the island of Hawaii. Its natural habitats are dry and montane moist forests, and it is the only bird species on the island to occupy the woodpecker niche. The bird is 5.5 inches (14 cm) in length, and has an unusually curved beak-(a specialist species).

children.name: Hemignathus munroi
children.description: Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Endangered fauna of Hawaii.

children.name: Hemignathus lucidus
children.description: Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Extinct birds of Hawaii.

children.name: Spiza
children.description: A grassland bird of the central USA; the sole member of its genus; winters in large flocks in South America.

children.name: Telespiza
children.description: Laysan and Nihoa finches of remote northwestern Hawaiian atolls, evolved as omnivorous survivors on tiny coral islands with limited resources.

children.name: Telespiza ultima
children.description: Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Critically endangered fauna of Hawaii.

children.name: Palmeria
children.description: The Akohekohe — one of the most spectacular Hawaiian honeycreepers, with an extraordinary crest and vivid orange-red plumage patches.

children.name: Palmeria dolei
children.description: The ʻākohekohe, or crested honeycreeper, is a species of Hawaiian honeycreeper. It is endemic to the island of Maui in Hawaiʻi. The ʻākohekohe is susceptible to mosquito‐transmitted avian malaria and only breeds in high‐elevation wet forests.

children.name: Pseudonestor
children.description: The Maui Parrotbill — a critically endangered Hawaiian honeycreeper with a massively powerful, parrot-like bill for tearing apart wood to find beetle larvae.

children.name: Pseudonestor xanthophrys
children.description: Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Critically endangered fauna of Hawaii.

children.name: Melamprosops
children.description: The Poo-uli — discovered in 1973, extinct by 2004. It represented a unique evolutionary lineage with no close relatives among known Hawaiian birds.

children.name: Melamprosops phaeosoma
children.description: Category:Carduelinae Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Endemic fauna of Hawaii Category:Critically endangered fauna of Hawaii Category:Taxa named by Tonnie Casey.

children.name: Oreomystis
children.description: The Akikiki — a tiny bark-creeping honeycreeper confined to Kauai's native forest, now one of the most critically endangered birds in the world.

children.name: Oreomystis bairdi
children.description: Category:Oreomystis Category:Endemic fauna of Hawaii.

children.name: Paroreomyza
children.description: Hawaiian creepers — small bark-foraging honeycreepers, several species of which are now extinct due to introduced rats and avian disease.

children.name: Paroreomyza maculata
children.description: Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Endemic fauna of Hawaii Category:Critically endangered fauna of Hawaii.

children.name: Magumma
children.description: The Anianiau — the smallest Hawaiian honeycreeper and one of the few with a relatively stable population, confined to Kauai's native forest.

children.name: Magumma parva
children.description: Category:Carduelinae Category:Hawaiian honeycreepers Category:Endemic fauna of Hawaii Category:Vulnerable fauna of Oceania.

children.name: Chloridops
children.description: The Kona Grosbeak — an extinct Hawaiian honeycreeper with an enormously massive bill for crushing the hard seeds of the naio tree, last seen in 1894.

children.name: Rhodacanthis
children.description: Koa finches — extinct Hawaiian honeycreepers with large bills adapted for crushing native koa pods, eliminated by introduced rats and forest clearance in the 19th century.

children.name: Loxioides
children.description: The Palila — a critically endangered Hawaiian honeycreeper entirely dependent on the mamane tree, and the subject of landmark environmental law cases in the USA.

children.name: Chlorodrepanis
children.description: Hawaiian amakihis — adaptable honeycreepers showing documented genetic resistance to avian malaria, allowing recolonisation of lower elevations than other honeycreepers can survive.

children.name: Rhodospiza
children.description: The Desert Finch — a distinctive pale finch of Central Asian and Middle Eastern arid zones; the sole member of its genus.

children.name: Callacanthis
children.description: The Spectacled Finch — a striking high-altitude finch of Himalayan conifer forests, the male bearing bold red supercilia and red-spotted wings; the sole member of its genus.

children.name: Pyrrhoplectes
children.description: The Gold-naped Finch — a jewel-like finch of dense Himalayan bamboo and rhododendron undergrowth, the male bearing a stunning gold-orange nape against jet-black plumage.

children.name: Haematospiza
children.description: The Scarlet Finch — a vivid scarlet finch of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests, the brilliantly coloured male one of the most eye-catching birds in the region.

children.name: Uragus
children.description: The Long-tailed Rosefinch — a delicate, pale pink finch of willow and birch scrub along rivers across Siberia and East Asia, with a distinctive long, forked tail.

children.name: Agraphospiza
children.description: The Crimson-browed Finch — a distinctive finch of dense Himalayan montane forest undergrowth, the male with a striking crimson forehead and orange-red breast.

children.name: Kozlowia
children.description: Roborowski's Rosefinch — a mysterious, little-known rosefinch of high-altitude Tibetan scrub, rarely observed even by experienced birders in the region.

children.name: Linurgus
children.description: The Oriole Finch — a distinctively coloured African finch of highland forests, the male combining vivid yellow plumage with a black head, resembling a miniature oriole.

children.name: Neospiza
children.description: The Sao Tome Grosbeak — a critically endangered, large-billed finch endemic to São Tomé, one of the rarest birds in Africa.

children.name: Rhynchostruthus
children.description: Golden-winged grosbeaks and the Warsangli Linnet — finches of the Horn of Africa and Socotra island with heavy bills and, in one species, vivid yellow wing patches.

children.name: Euphonia
children.description: Euphonias — vivid Neotropical finches now placed in Fringillidae by genomic data. Males are typically deep blue-black above with vivid yellow underparts. Specialists on mistletoe berries, they have evolved rapid gut passage to excrete seeds before toxins are absorbed, making them critical mistletoe dispersers in Neotropical forests.

children.name: Euphonia fulvicrissa
children.description: The fulvous-vented euphonia is a species of bird in the family Fringillidae, the finches and euphonias. It is found in Colombia, Ecuador, and Panama.

children.name: Chlorophonia
children.description: Chlorophonias — vivid emerald-green Neotropical finches closely related to euphonias, now classified within Fringillidae. Males are brilliant green with yellow or blue highlights; females predominantly olive. Chlorophonias inhabit montane forests from Mexico to northern South America.
